question: The patient underwent surgical debridement with an apically repositioned flap and was sutured with absorbable polyglycolic acid sutures. Postoperative pharmacologic therapy is being planned. Based on the clinical scenario and the image, which of the following is most appropriate?

options.A: Short-course NSAID plus chlorhexidine mouthrinse (no systemic antibiotics)
options.E: Topical antibiotic gel applied to the surgical site

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image for objective findings. The photo shows the lower anterior dentition after flap surgery: multiple apically repositioned mucosal flaps are sutured in place with interrupted absorbable sutures, there is expected fresh blood and coagulum along the wound margins, and the soft tissues are approximated without obvious pus, extensive edema, or signs of spreading infection.

Next, I relate what I see to the question, which asks which postoperative pharmacologic approach is most appropriate after this surgical debridement and apically repositioned flap. Key visual points are a primarily closed surgical site with intact sutures and no gross signs of active infection.

Then, I consider how each option aligns with those observations. Systemic antibiotics (options B, C, D) would be most justifiable when there is clinical evidence of infection or high risk for systemic spread; that evidence is not visible in this image. A topical antibiotic gel (option E) would be an additional local antibiotic application to an already approximated and clean-appearing wound and is not clearly indicated by the appearance. Option A offers local antiseptic control (chlorhexidine mouthrinse) plus an analgesic/anti-inflammatory agent (short-course NSAID) without systemic antibiotics, which directly addresses postoperative pain/inflammation and local plaque control while avoiding unnecessary systemic therapy.

Therefore, based on the image of a clean, well-approximated surgical site without signs of infection, the most appropriate postoperative pharmacologic plan is short-course NSAID plus chlorhexidine mouthrinse (no systemic antibiotics).
caption: Case 1. The clinical views of modified gingivoplasty. For the mandible, the full thickness flap was raised with the crevicular incision a The fibrotic connective tissue was entirely removed according to its colour and texture b The osteoplasty was applied to remove the fibrotic alveolar bone c After the debridement, the flap was repositioned apically and sutured d For the maxilla, Intraoral views(central, right and left occlusal views of maxilla) e, g, h; Central maxillary palatal views f
sub_caption: After the debridement, the flap was repositioned apically and sutured d
image_context: On the basis of these findings, a diagnosis of non-syndromic hereditary gingival fibromatosis (HGF) was given. With the consent of the patient and her family, non-surgical periodontal treatment which comprised sub-gingival scaling and root planning and oral hygiene instruction was initially taken. Next, a bilateral posterior superior alveolar nerve block was administered with a supplemental local anesthesia obtained through labial and palatal infiltration. After strict disinfection, gingivoplasty surgery in both labial/buccal and palatal/lingual sides in the maxilla was performed. Crevicular incision was made along to the sulcus the using a cconventional scalpel (11/12/15c) and down to the alveolus and then, full thickness flap was raised above the mucogingival junction (Fig. <xref rid="12903_2022_2411_Fig4_HTML" ref-type="fig">4</xref>a). The fibrotic connective tissue and alveolar bone were exposed. It’s not difficult to distinguish the pathological tissue from the outside soft tissue, for the pathological part is very hard in texture and pale in colour. Therefore, depending on the texture and color, the fibrotic connective tissue was resected and the gingiva was scalloped in shape, with slightly thinner margins (Fig. a). The fibrotic connective tissue and alveolar bone were exposed. It’s not difficult to distinguish the pathological tissue from the outside soft tissue, for the pathological part is very hard in texture and pale in colour. Therefore, depending on the texture and color, the fibrotic connective tissue was resected and the gingiva was scalloped in shape, with slightly thinner margins (Fig. <xref rid="12903_2022_2411_Fig4_HTML" ref-type="fig">4</xref>b). The handpiece and bur were used to remove the outside fibrotic alveolar bone and reshape normal physiological alveolar anatomy (Fig. b). The handpiece and bur were used to remove the outside fibrotic alveolar bone and reshape normal physiological alveolar anatomy (Fig. <xref rid="12903_2022_2411_Fig4_HTML" ref-type="fig">4</xref>c). The split thickness gingival flap was raised below the mucogingival junction. After debridement, the flap was repositioned apically and sutured with absorbable suture (polyglycolic acid braid with short term resorption) (Fig. c). The split thickness gingival flap was raised below the mucogingival junction. After debridement, the flap was repositioned apically and sutured with absorbable suture (polyglycolic acid braid with short term resorption) (Fig. <xref rid="12903_2022_2411_Fig4_HTML" ref-type="fig">4</xref>d). With a bilateral inferior alveolar nerve block anesthesia and labial and palatal infiltration anesthesia, the operation in the mandible was utilized 4 weeks after the one in the maxilla (Fig. d). With a bilateral inferior alveolar nerve block anesthesia and labial and palatal infiltration anesthesia, the operation in the mandible was utilized 4 weeks after the one in the maxilla (Fig. <xref rid="12903_2022_2411_Fig4_HTML" ref-type="fig">4</xref>e–h). The patient was advised to continue taking the antibiotic (amoxicillin, 500 mg tds) and fluid intake after operation for 3 days. Intense exercise and brushing teeth within 24 h after operation should be avoided. A 0.12% chlorhexidine gluconate rinse was prescribed for administration twice a day for one week. The patient could eat without pain 7 days after the operation. Comfortably eating was achieved 14 days after the suture were removed. Complete healing occurred 3 months after the operation.e–h). The patient was advised to continue taking the antibiotic (amoxicillin, 500 mg tds) and fluid intake after operation for 3 days. Intense exercise and brushing teeth within 24 h after operation should be avoided. A 0.12% chlorhexidine gluconate rinse was prescribed for administration twice a day for one week. The patient could eat without pain 7 days after the operation. Comfortably eating was achieved 14 days after the suture were removed.
